dialogue.utterance: Emergency Unit 3 dispatched for a solo call for chest pain at Grace House in Richmond.
Hi there, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I am having chest pain on the right side that has been going on for a full week.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
I'm Diana-would you believe this is our home in Richmond?
Do you know what day it is today?
I… I know it's July, but not the exact day.
Great. How about your name and location? Can you tell me that again?
I'm Diana… and we're at Grace House.
You are oriented to person and place, which is good. Your GCS is 15, and you are AOX3 per Grace House staff baseline.
Your chin is forward, which is good for your airway. Can you speak clearly?
Yes, I can speak clearly.
Are you having trouble breathing?
No, I don't feel too out of breath.
Partner, can you check her pulse and blood pressure while I further assess her breathing?
Pulse is 80-regular and strong.
Respirations are at 16, non-labored.
Blood pressure is 152 over 83. Her pulse is regular.
O2 saturation is 97% on room air-good levels.
Blood glucose is 126 mg/dL-within the normal range.
ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm-no signs of irregularity.
Your skin is warm, dry, intact, and without any rashes or bruising.
Your pupils appear approximately 4 mm and equal-PEARL. Do you have any facial drooping or difficulty moving your face?
No, my face feels fine.
No jugular venous distention is identified. Your neck veins are unremarkable.
Your lungs are clear, and your chest rise and fall looks normal. Are you experiencing pain only on the right side, as you mentioned?
Yes, just on the right side and not with my breathing.
Our staff has noted you are ambulatory and moving well. Any pain in arms, legs, or back?
No pain at all in the limbs-just can't chew.
Peripheral motor strength is present and equal in all extremities.
Can you point to where exactly you feel the chest pain?
On a scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst pain, how would you rate it now?
Does the pain go anywhere else?
10. It doesn't spread-just stays here like a knife.
Can you confirm your age and gender for our records?
I'm 53 and female.
What medications are you currently taking regularly?
Amlodipine for blood pressure and also Celexa, Haldol, Hydrochlorothiazide, and Oxcarbazepine.
Have you ever had any allergies to drugs or food?
No-never any allergic reactions.
Do you recall what your last meal was and when you had it?
I remember having soup for dinner, but I'm not sure the exact time.
We did a 12-lead EKG and it showed a borderline left axis deviation, but nothing to worry about.
We placed you on an IV in your left arm and started a four-lead ECG monitor.
We usually give aspirin, but you mentioned you can't chew. We'll skip that one for now.
Pulse is now 71, still regular. Respirations remain at 16, non-labored.
Blood pressure is 144 over 75 now, and O2 is 99%.
EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm-no new changes.
IV and monitor still intact-no issues so far.
Your pain is still at 10 on a 10 scale and not radiating. It doesn't seem to be due to breathing.
We are following the Medical-Chest Pain-Cardiac Suspected protocol (Protocol 2-1).
We'll transport you to St. Mary's ER. Is that okay with you?
Yes, take me when you're ready.
We're going to get you onto the stretcher now so we can wheel you to the ambulance.
She walked outside to the stretcher on her own, sat down and didn't resist.
She's secured to the stretcher and ready to go. Vitals are monitored continuously.
Loading into the ambulance now. I'll radio the hospital with your condition.
She's settled in back, all belts fastened.
Smooth sail-like a routine I get every week. Calm.
She's breathing well and has no facial drooping.
On our way to St. Mary's. ED is notified and prepared for arrival.
She will go straight to triage, where she will be given further care.
No legal issues with transport-documented and signed.
She maintains orientation and is alert to name and location.
I see you have a history of Paranoid Personality Disorder and Schizoaffective Disorder, noted from Grace House. Is that correct?
Yes, they say I have those conditions.
Peripheral motor strengths are symmetrical and equal in all extremities.
No signs of jugular venous distention and tracheal deviation noted.
No deterioration, no pain, and all systems nominal so far.
Per protocol, we're transporting to the emergency department for further evaluation.
And we have documented your medical history of Paranoid Personality Disorder and Schizoaffective Disorder.
Peripheral motor strength is assessed as equivalent in all four limbs.
I have continued monitoring ongoing O2 saturation and EKG for the duration.
Your current blood pressure is 144/75 and your breathing is still at 16.
I will continue to monitor during the transport and repeat assessments as needed.
All medical interventions are properly documented, and no additional procedures are indicated at this time.
Your pulse remains at 71 and steady, and your O2 is at 99%.
I'm keeping your IV secured and making sure the EKG monitor remains connected.
We will begin with transmitting your vitals and EKG to the ER now.
Your transfer will be safe, and the hospital is expecting you.
No evidence of jugular venous distention. Your neck remains soft and supple.
No allergies reported in the record or mentioned by you-perfect.
Peripheral motor strength and pulses are present and equal. No skin abnormalities found.
No acute deterioration thus far. Condition remains stable on both vitals and pain.
Your IV site is continuing to work fine-no infiltration or complication.
Your motor and sensory function remains intact, and your mobility has been consistent.
You're in good hands here, and we're moving as smoothly as possible.
Let's review one more time before arrival-pain is at 10, no radiation, on the right side.
All equipment secure and no incident during transport-ready to proceed.
You're very knowledgeable about these routines, but we will be maintaining standard monitoring.
Our vitals are consistent, the EKG is ongoing, and the IV is running.
We are nearing the hospital and will prepare for handover now.
Let's check the stretcher once again to confirm your positioning and comfort level.
No signs of instability, pain flare-ups, or any acute change in condition.
Your vital signs and pain level have not changed during transport.
Keeping track of your regularly occurring meal notes-had soup, no known time, as per Grace House staff.
She remains stable in both oxygen and blood pressure throughout the trip.
Vital signs and EKG have remained within acceptable parameters during transport.
All baseline documentation is核实 completed and waiting for hospital sign-off.
All going well for a smooth handoff at the ED.
